title: Today's AI Brief: Catch up on 12 major I/O 2026 moments
summary: A relaxed rundown of the latest AI news, covering Google I/O 2026 highlights and the implications of AI on society.

body.text: I'm Leo Stone, and welcome to your AI Brief. I've got my eyes on the freshest news in the world of artificial intelligence. First up, Google's I/O 2026 conference just wrapped up, and there were some major announcements. The company has posted a recap of 12 key moments from the event, including the debut of their newest models and updates to Search.

One highlight from the conference was the unveiling of Google's latest AI models. While I won't get into the nitty-gritty details, it's clear that these models are designed to push the boundaries of what's possible with AI. Another interesting moment was the update to Search, which promises to make finding what you need even easier. I'm curious to see how these updates will change the way I (and you) interact with information online.

But AI isn't all sunshine and rainbows. An article from Axios caught my eye, suggesting that AI might be ushering in a new era of colonialism. The idea is that AI systems, which are often built and controlled by a handful of powerful companies, could end up concentrating even more power and influence in the hands of a few. It's a sobering thought, and one that I think we'll be hearing more about in the months to come.

My quick read: the artificial intelligence story keeps getting bigger, but also stranger in very human ways. One moment it is strategy and infrastructure; the next, the Vatican is in the room. That is a pretty good sign this technology has left the lab and wandered into the town square.

sources.title: Catch up on 12 major I/O 2026 moments
AI is ushering in a new era of colonialism - Axios
